data.title: FVCbank to Honor America’s 250th Anniversary with Year-Long “America250” Initiative
data.description:
FVCbank to Honor America’s 250th Anniversary with Year-Long “America250” Initiative

As the United States approaches the historic milestone of its 250th anniversary in 2026, FVCbank today announced the launch of America250, a year-long initiative designed to honor the enduring values that have shaped the nation for nearly two and a half centuries.

Through a thoughtful series of social media posts, website highlights, radio spots, and other marketing channels, FVCbank will reflect on the principles of leadership, integrity, enterprise, stewardship, and trust that guided the nation’s Founders and continue to define American progress today.

A cornerstone of the initiative is “13 Stars of America,” a values-based series recognizing thirteen of the nation’s original Founders. Each installment highlights a leadership principle embodied at the founding of the United States and explores its relevance in today’s business and community environment.

“America’s 250th anniversary is an opportunity to pause and reflect on the values that have endured across generations,” said David W. Pijor, Chairman and CEO of FVCbank. “Those same principles—trust, discipline, innovation, and service—remain essential to strong institutions and healthy communities.”

Patricia A. Ferrick, President of FVCbank, added, “As a community-focused bank, we see America250 as a moment to connect history with the present, honoring the leadership ideals that continue to guide how we serve our clients and our communities.”

The America250 initiative will be featured throughout 2026 across FVCbank’s communications channels, including LinkedIn, radio programming, branch materials, and select commemorative elements. All content is designed to be educational, inclusive, and non-political, emphasizing shared values rather than ideology.

Founded on a commitment to relationship-driven banking, FVCbank views America250 as a natural extension of its mission to support long-term growth, community engagement, and responsible leadership.

About FVCbank

FVCbank is a community-focused financial institution serving businesses, professionals, and communities throughout Northern Virginia, Maryland, and the greater Washington, D.C. region. The bank is committed to delivering responsive service, thoughtful solutions, and long-term value for its clients and communities.

Bruce Gemmill, Chief Marketing Officer
Email: bgemmill@fvcbank.com
Cell: 571-379-6414

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260123747893/en/

data.title: Nathan’s Famous Investor Alert: Kahn Swick & Foti, LLC Investigates Adequacy of Price and Process in Proposed Sale of Nathan’s Famous, Inc. - NATH
data.description:
Nathan’s Famous Investor Alert: Kahn Swick & Foti, LLC Investigates Adequacy of Price and Process in Proposed Sale of Nathan’s Famous, Inc. - NATH

Former Attorney General of Louisiana Charles C. Foti, Jr., Esq. and the law firm of Kahn Swick & Foti, LLC (“KSF”) are investigating the proposed sale of Nathan’s Famous, Inc. (NasdaqGS: NATH) to Smithfield Foods, Inc. (NasdaqGS: SFD). Under the terms of the proposed transaction, shareholders of Nathan’s will receive $102.00 in cash for each share of Nathan’s that they own. KSF is seeking to determine whether this consideration and the process that led to it are adequate, or whether the consideration undervalues the Company.

If you believe that this transaction undervalues the Company and/or if you would like to discuss your legal rights regarding the proposed sale, you may, without obligation or cost to you, e-mail or call KSF Managing Partner Lewis S. Kahn (lewis.kahn@ksfcounsel.com) toll free at any time at 855-768-1857, or visit https://www.ksfcounsel.com/cases/nasdaqgs-nath/ to learn more.

To learn more about KSF, whose partners include the Former Louisiana Attorney General, visit www.ksfcounsel.com.

CONNECT WITH US: Facebook || Instagram || YouTube || TikTok || LinkedIn

Kahn Swick & Foti, LLC
Lewis S. Kahn, KSF Managing Partner

data.title: Ice Canola Posting Small Gains Friday Morning
data.description:
WINNIPEG, Manitoba--ICE canola futures posting small gains Friday morning, continuing their steady uptrend.

* Gains in Chicago soyoil provided spillover support, with that market at its highest levels in five months.

* Soybeans and European rapeseed futures were also higher, although Malaysian palm oil was lower.

* Canada exported 288,200 metric tons of canola during the week ended Jan. 18, which was up from 112,600 tons the previous week. Crop year-to-date exports of about 3.2 million tons are roughly 2.1 million tons off the year ago pace.

* Optimism that exports will pick up when Chinese tariffs are lowered in March after a deal was reached last week remained supportive.

* About 23,000 canola contracts had traded as of 9:16 EST.

Prices in Canadian dollars per metric ton at 9:16 EST: 

 
           Price      Change 
Mar       649.30     up 1.90 
May       659.10     up 1.40 
Jul       666.10     up 1.50 
Nov       657.80     up 1.60

Source: Commodity News Service Canada, news@marketsfarm.com 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  January 23, 2026 10:43 ET (15:43 GMT)

data.title: Intel delivers a reality check after stock surge fueled by 'vibes and tweets'
data.description:
By Britney Nguyen

Intel's stock heads for its worst drop in a year and a half as earnings commentary fails to meet investors' lofty expectations

Intel's stock was down more than 14% after the company reported fourth-quarter earnings.

The lofty ideas underpinning Intel's recent stock surge just collided with the reality of a company still very much in transition.

In the three weeks leading up to Thursday's earnings report, Intel's stock (INTC) climbed 47%, "mostly on vibes and tweets," Bernstein analyst Stacy Rasgon wrote. Therefore, Intel's results "had to be perfect," he said, and they were not.

Intel is indeed benefiting from ample interest in server central processing units, which is one of the trends that had investors so excited ahead of earnings. But the company "appears to have woefully misjudged" demand, Rasgon said, leading its supply capacity to be "caught massively off guard."

And while the company pointed to progress in its manufacturing business, management doesn't expect customers to decide on whether they want to use Intel's forthcoming 14A technology until the second half of this year.

Don't miss: Micron's business is so hot that profits could quadruple in just two years, says this analyst

In short, the parts of Intel's business that investors had been enthusiastic about "are still there in theory," but not to the extent that could support the stock's strong run, Rasgon wrote in a Thursday note.

Shares of Intel are off 14.2% in morning trading Friday, coursing toward their worst daily drop since Aug. 2, 2024, when they lost 26.1%, according to Dow Jones Market Data.

Wedbush analyst Matt Bryson suggested that some of Intel's commentary shouldn't have surprised investors, as management hinted at potential March-quarter struggles three months ago. Nonetheless, expectations "got ahead of Intel's reality," he said in a Friday note.

While this month's launch of Intel's Panther Lake processors - the first built on the 18A node - was a positive development, Bryson said he still finds it difficult to understand what the long-term financial benefit will be for the business.

Bryson is keeping a measured view of Intel's stock, as is TD Cowen analyst Joshua Buchalter, who chimed in that the recent stock appreciation was "largely driven by 'the dream' rather than the near-term reality or fundamentals."

While he is skeptical about how much Intel can change in the near term, it's "still too early to fully count Intel out," he said.

For now, Buchalter sees challenges ahead for Intel's personal-computer and server segments as the company faces competition from Advanced Micro Devices (AMD).

More from MarketWatch: Big Tech stocks haven't been this cheap in months. These investors say it's time to buy.

-Britney Nguyen

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal. 




  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  01-23-26 1036ET


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Business Activity Picks Up in Parts of Europe and Asia — 3rd Update
data.description:
By Paul Hannon 




  Business activity in the U.S. and a number of large economies across Europe and Asia picked up at the start of the year, with signs of a rebound in export orders as the global economy adjusts to the increase in U.S. tariffs. 



  Data firm S&P Global said Friday that its purchasing managers index measure of activity at manufacturers and service providers in the U.S. rose to 52.8 in January from 52.7 in December. A reading below 50.0 signals a decline in activity, and a reading above that level indicates an expansion. 



  Similar surveys pointed to accelerations in India, Japan and Australia. 



  The global economy grew more rapidly than expected in 2025, as a U.S. boom in artificial-intelligence-related investment helped offset the hit of higher tariffs. 



  In recent reports, the International Monetary Fund and the World Bank have each raised their growth forecasts for this year. However, they warned that growth could be slower if tariffs rise again or the AI boom runs out of steam. 



  "We are in a more shock-prone world," IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva said during a panel at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland. 



  However, the outlook for Europe remains relatively weak, with growth set to lag behind the U.S. and China. Also speaking at Davos, European Central Bank President Christine Lagarde said she had heard a lot of "Europe-bashing" during a week of meetings, but welcomed that as an incentive to do better. 



  "We have to focus on innovation and productivity," she said. 



  The PMI for the eurozone was unchanged at 51.5 in January. However, that reflected a drop in activity in France, while growth accelerated in Germany and continued in other parts of the currency area. 



  Businesses reported a strengthening of overseas demand as 2026 got under way. In Australia, export orders rose at the fastest pace in three-and-a-half years, while in Japan they increased for the first time since March. 



  "January data showed a marked upturn in aggregate international orders," S&P Global said of India's performance. "Asia, Australia, Europe, Latin America and the Middle East featured as the main destinations for Indian goods and services in the latest month." 



  As an economy that has relied on exports of manufactured goods as a driver of growth this century, Germany was hit hard by the rise in U.S. tariffs, although its economy avoided a contraction last year. 



  The survey of purchasing managers pointed to a stabilization in export orders, allowing overall demand to increase and supporting the largest rise in activity in three months. 



  In the U.K., export orders rose at the fastest pace in 18 months, with the PMI hitting its highest level since April 2024. 



  By contrast, U.S. businesses reported the largest drop in overseas orders since April 2025, when President Trump announced higher tariffs on imports from most of the country's trading partners. 





  Write to Paul Hannon at paul.hannon@wsj.com 





  (END) Dow Jones Newswires



  January 23, 2026 10:36 ET (15:36 GMT)


Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.

data.title: Avangrid Utilities Prepare for Potential Impacts from Winter Storm Fern
data.description:
Avangrid Utilities Prepare for Potential Impacts from Winter Storm Fern

Potential severe weather including extreme cold and snow forecasted across Northeast region

Avangrid’s major grid investments continue to strengthen system for enhanced storm response and recovery

Avangrid, Inc., a leading energy company and member of the Iberdrola Group, today announced that its utility companies, New York State Electric & Gas (NYSEG) and Rochester Gas and Electric (RG&E), Central Maine Power (CMP) and United Illuminating (UI), continue to monitor and prepare for the potential impacts of severe winter weather, including frigid cold, expected across the Northeast this weekend.

This press release features multimedia. View the full release here: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260123515082/en/

Pictured: Avangrid crews respond to winter storm. Photo courtesy of Avangrid.

“Avangrid continues to make significant investments toward strengthening and modernizing the grid, which puts our utility companies in a better position to prepare for and respond to severe weather events,” said Avangrid CEO Jose Antonio Miranda. “As we continue monitoring the weather patterns and likely track of Winter Storm Fern, Avangrid personnel will be ready to respond as needed to any impacts in the communities we serve.”

In advance of the storm, Avangrid’s storm readiness crews are monitoring weather forecasts, planning, readying equipment, and mobilizing employees for storm duty. Crews will be strategically positioned and staffed around the clock to help restore service should outages occur.

Avangrid’s preparation builds on significant investments made throughout 2025 to improve system resilience. Last year, Avangrid’s utility companies installed over 650 smart devices on the grid and replaced 24,000 utility poles. Smart devices provide energy control centers with live readings of the grid, letting operators remotely respond to outages and restore service in as little as five minutes. Many utility poles in Avangrid’s service territory across the Northeast are decades old, making the grid more vulnerable to storm damage and outages. Replacing these aging poles is critical to improving resiliency and energy reliability as extreme weather events become more frequent and severe.

Avangrid stands ready to move forward with the next wave of critical infrastructure investments that are essential to maintaining reliability and meeting the region’s growing energy demands, subject to approval from relevant state regulatory entities. Pending regulatory approval, Avangrid’s investments will help keep the lights on for millions of families across the northeast while advancing economic development, creating jobs, and building prosperous communities.

About Avangrid: Avangrid, Inc. is a leading energy company in the United States working to meet the growing demand for energy for homes and businesses across the nation through service, innovation, and continued investments by expanding grid infrastructure and energy generation projects. Avangrid has offices in Connecticut, New York, Massachusetts, Maine, and Oregon, including operations in 23 states with approximately $48 billion in assets, and has two primary lines of business: networks and power. Through its networks business, Avangrid owns and operates eight electric and natural gas utilities, serving more than 3.4 million customers in New York and New England. Through its power generation business, Avangrid owns and operates 80 energy generation facilities across the United States producing 10.5 GW of power for over 3.1 million customers. Avangrid employs approximately 8,000 people and has been recognized by JUST Capital as one of the JUST 100 companies – a ranking of America’s best corporate citizens – in 2025 for the fifth consecutive year. The company was named among the World’s Most Ethical Companies in 2025 for the seventh consecutive year by the Ethisphere Institute. Avangrid is a member of the group of companies controlled by Iberdrola, S.A. For more information, visit http://www.avangrid.com. 

Media Contact:
Florencia Feleder
florencia.feleder@avangrid.com
838-292-6869

View source version on businesswire.com: https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260123515082/en/

data.title: Yes, you still need an emergency fund in retirement. Here's how much you should have.
data.description:
By Robert Powell

Many retirees aren't prepared to handle unexpected expenses

Retirees tend to face larger spending shocks than workers, often driven by unpredictable costs such as healthcare.

Financial planners often suggest that before you start saving and investing for retirement, for a down payment on a house or for your children's college fund, you first establish an emergency fund.

That advice makes sense for working-age households, which may face income shocks such as job loss, disability or reduced pay, as well as spending shocks such as unexpected major home repairs.

Emergency savings are meant to provide a buffer that helps households weather those shocks and avoid relying on high-cost borrowing, such as credit cards.

The rule of thumb is well-known: Families are typically told to set aside three to six months' worth of essential expenses to cover worst-case scenarios and other surprises.

For people who are still working, that guidance is straightforward.

But what about those on retirement's doorstep, or already in retirement? Do they still need an emergency fund, and if so, how much should they have - three to six months' worth of expenses, 10% of their income, enough to cover a year or more of spending, or some other amount?

A new study from the Center for Retirement Research at Boston College tackles that question and examines how prepared retirees are to handle unexpected expenses.

In their study - titled "How Much Are Emergency Expenses for Retirees and Are They Prepared?" - researchers Manita Rao and Anqi Chen reached a sobering conclusion: Many retirees are ill-prepared to handle unexpected expenses.

The typical retired household spends about 10% of income on unexpected expenses in a normal year, Rao and Chen found. Yet 40% of retirees lack the cash to cover even one year's worth of such expenses. One in five falls short after including retirement savings, and 27% cannot cover a single year of unexpected expenses even after exhausting both cash and retirement accounts.

"Unexpected expenses do not go away once people retire," the authors wrote, calling them a real and persistent risk for retired households.

Those expenses can be a significant source of financial stress, particularly for vulnerable groups, including lower-income households, Black and Hispanic households, single women and widowed retirees.

So what guidance do experts offer?

Plan explicitly for emergency expenses in retirement

Sharon Carson, a retirement strategist with J.P. Morgan Asset Management, said the researchers' findings align closely with her firm's own research, which highlights both the variability of spending in retirement and the continued need for emergency savings after someone stops working.

In its 2025 Guide to Retirement, J.P. Morgan Asset Management examined how much net income workers ages 25 to 64 and retirees 65 and older need in order to weather spending and income shocks.

Workers typically encounter spending shocks more frequently - about once every three months - than income shocks, which tend to occur about once a year. As a result, workers are generally advised to set aside two to three months of pay in an emergency fund, the firm noted.

Retirees, by contrast, tend to face larger spending shocks than workers, often driven by unpredictable costs such as healthcare. For that reason, retirees should consider holding three to six months' worth of income in emergency savings, according to J.P. Morgan (JPM).

The following chart shows the recommended annual emergency reserves, expressed in the number of weeks of net income that are needed to weather spending and income shocks: 

   Gross income                                                                                                                                                                           Workers (ages 25-64)  Retirees (ages 65 and over) 
   <$50,000                                                                                                                                                                               5-14 weeks            8-22 weeks 
   $50,000-$90,000                                                                                                                                                                        4-10 weeks            8-19 weeks 
   $90,000-$150,000                                                                                                                                                                       4-10 weeks            8-19 weeks 
   $150,000-$1 million                                                                                                                                                                    5-12 weeks            8-20 weeks 
   Percentiles: The lower values (e.g., 4-8 weeks) represent the 50th percentile (median) of the population, while the higher values (e.g., 10-22 weeks) represent the 75th percentile.

There are two main reasons retirees need to have more income set aside, Carson said. First, they may face larger healthcare costs and more significant housing repairs, particularly those living in older homes. Second, income often declines in retirement, so when savings are measured relative to net income, more weeks of income are required.

The Center for Retirement Research also found that the nature of financial shocks changes in retirement.

Job loss is the largest financial shock for working households. In retirement, health-related, housing-related and family-related expenses play a larger role.

As many as 60% of households experience a "rainy-day" shock, such as car repairs, home maintenance or other durable purchases, Rao and Chen found. About 29% face unexpected family-related expenses, including transfers to parents or children, the death of a spouse or divorce. And 58% encounter unexpected healthcare costs, ranging from dental care and prescription drugs to home modifications, nursing-home care or in-home care.

In dollar terms, the average predicted amount of unexpected expenses across all households is about $6,000 per year, with healthcare the largest category for retired households, according to the study.

For planning purposes, Rao and Chen wrote that retirees should consider having emergency savings equal to roughly 10% of annual income. Over a 25-year retirement, that translates into unexpected expenses totaling about 2.5 years' worth of income, although those costs are spread out over time and the full amount would not need to be held in a liquid account.

Expect spending volatility to persist

Research by Sudipto Banerjee, director of retirement thought leadership at T. Rowe Price, points to substantial year-to-year swings. About one in four retirees experienced an increase of roughly 17% to 20% in annual spending over a two-year period, while a similar share experienced a comparable decline over the same time frame, he found.

Housing plays an outsize role in that volatility. While healthcare and family-related costs often drive discrete spending shocks, housing and home-related expenses are the largest contributors to year-to-year swings in retirement spending.

Given that, retirees should consider steps to reduce the likelihood of unexpected housing expenses, such as completing major repairs before retirement or downsizing to a newer home - moves that can decrease the need for emergency cash later on, Banerjee said.

Do not rely solely on retirement accounts for emergencies

When households lack dedicated emergency savings, they often tap retirement portfolios to cover unexpected costs, the J.P. Morgan research showed.

That approach can be costly. "Prematurely taking money out of retirement savings accounts can have dire consequences in achieving a successful retirement outcome," the firm's Guide to Retirement noted.

Spending increases are not always temporary. About 15% of households that experienced a spending increase of 25% or more were still spending at elevated levels four years later, creating liquidity challenges that can force retirees to draw down long-term investments sooner than planned, Banerjee found.

Strategies that can help older adults manage unexpected expenses include delaying claiming Social Security, improving advice about drawing down retirement accounts and making greater use of health savings accounts, Rao and Chen said.

How much should be in your emergency fund?

When it comes to estimating how much emergency savings retirees should have, the guidance can appear inconsistent at first.

J.P. Morgan recommends that retirees hold three to six months' worth of income in emergency savings. Rao and Chen suggest setting aside 10% of annual income. And some experts, including Judy Ward, who recently retired from T. Rowe Price, suggest that retirees consider holding one to two years' worth of spending in cash or cash equivalents to guard against prolonged disruptions and avoid forced withdrawals from long-term portfolios during periods of stress.

Taken together, however, the guidance points to a common theme: Retirees need enough liquid savings to weather the next shock as well as a broader plan that treats unexpected expenses as a routine part of retirement rather than a one-time event.

Exactly how much is enough for any individual household depends on how often retirees face expense shocks, the size of those shocks and how prepared they are today.

And regardless of how much is set aside, timing matters. Because households are likely to face unexpected spending in virtually every year of retirement, retirees should build up their emergency savings early.

"Since retirees have a limited ability to replenish their savings through additional years of working, having as much of these resources as possible at the beginning of retirement is important for their financial security," Rao and Chen advised.

-Robert Powell

(MORE TO FOLLOW) Dow Jones Newswires

01-23-26 1019ET

Copyright (c) 2026 Dow Jones & Company, Inc.
